messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2024.pdf
Equipment class: valve

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

Higher Δt coils require less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "valve_authority", "title": "Valve Authority for HVAC Coil Applications", "summary": "Valve authority in the range of 30 to 50% is adequate for HVAC coil applications with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios. Higher Δt coils require less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)
• System integration with other building systems
• Other HV AC goals• Security and environmental health and safety criteria• Building envelope• Project timeline (design, equipment delivery, commissioning,
training, and construction)
• Basis of design• HV AC system analysis and selection matrix• System narratives• Budget costs (first cost, operating cost, and energy cost)• Final recommendation(s)
A brief outline of each of the final selections should be provided
along with overall budget and/or cost per square metre. In addition,
HV AC systems deemed inappropriate should be noted as havingbeen considered but not found applicable to meet the owner’s pri-mary HVAC goal.
The report should include an HV AC system selection matrix that
identifies the one or two suggested HV AC system selections (pri -
mary and secondary, when applicable), system constraints, and
other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_82be96182b15f715", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Control Valve Sizing Basis", "summary": "Control valves should be sized based on the required valve coefficient Cv for the required pressure drop and flow.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "control_valve_sizing", "title": "Control Valve Sizing Basis", "summary": "Control valves should be sized based on the required valve coefficient Cv for the required pressure drop and flow."}, "evidence": "13.16 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment (SI)

less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided
because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature (Figures 27 and
28). For the three-way valve shown in Figure 23, the full-open pres-
sure drop should be from C to D.  The pressure drop in the bypass
balancing valve in the three-way valv e circuit should be set to equal
that in the coil (load), Chapter 47 discusses valve authority in threeway modulating applications.

coefficient C

information on calculating a dehumidi fying coil’s sensible heat ratio,
see the section on Performance of Dehumidifying Coils, or Appen-dix D of Cooling and Heating Load Calculation Principles  (Peder-
sen et al. 1998). For a given coil surface design and arrangement, therequired sensible heat ratio may be  satisfied by wide variations in
and combinations of air face velocity, in-tube temperature, flow rate,entering air temperature, coil depth, etc., although the variationsmay be self limiting. The maximum coil air face velocity should belimited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork.Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selectedin the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
Operating ratings of dehumidifyi ng coils for factory-assembled,
self-contained air conditioners are generally determined in conjunc-
tion with laboratory testing for the system capacity of the complete
unit assembly.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_d15238d69c159808", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Water Temperatures", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "supply_water_temperature", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Water Temperatures", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

For ethylene glycol solution, it is 0.3 to 1.8 m/s.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water_velocity", "title": "Coil Rating Water Velocity Range", "summary": "Water velocity for coil ratings is 0.3 to 2.4 m/s. For ethylene glycol solution, it is 0.3 to 1.8 m/s."}, "evidence": "The final choice
can then be made based on sy stem performance and economic
requirements.
Performance and Ratings
The long-term performance of an extended-surface air-cooling
and dehumidifying coil depends on it s correct design to specified
conditions and material specificat ions, proper matching to other
system components, proper instal lation, and proper maintenance as
required.
In accordance with AHRI Standard 410, dry-surface (sensible
cooling) coils and dehumidifying coils (which both cool and dehu-
midify), particularly those for fiel d-assembled coil banks or factory-
assembled packaged units using different combinations of coils, areusually rated within the following parameters:
Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 18 to 38°C
Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 15 to 30°C (if air is not dehu-
midified in the application, se lect coils based on sensible heat
transfer)
Air face velocity: 1 to 4 m/sEvaporator refrigerant saturation temperature: –1 to 13°C at coil
suction outlet (refrigerant vapor superheat at co il suction out-
let is 3 K or higher)
Entering chilled-water te mperature: 1.5 to 18°C
Water velocity: 0.3 to 2.4 m/s
For ethylene glycol solution: 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, –18 to 32°C entering
dry-bulb temperature, 15 to 27°C entering wet-bulb tempera-
ture, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass
The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidi-
fying coils varies in practice from ab out 0.6 to 1.0 (i.e., sensible heat
is from 60 to 100% of the total, depending on the application).", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_4c0cf1153f927aa0", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Dehumidifying Coil Sensible Heat Ratio Range", "summary": "The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidifying coils varies from about 0.6 to 1.0 (sensible heat is 60 to 100% of total).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "sensible_heat_ratio", "title": "Dehumidifying Coil Sensible Heat Ratio Range", "summary": "The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidifying coils varies from about 0.6 to 1.0 (sensible heat is 60 to 100% of total)."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

suction. For water coil s, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C

fort operations.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_35c6281e6609d74a", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Refrigerant Superheat for Standard Rating", "summary": "Refrigerant duty for standard rating would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "refrigerant_superheat", "title": "Refrigerant Superheat for Standard Rating", "summary": "Refrigerant duty for standard rating would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction."}, "evidence": "For example, a standard rating point has been

effect). The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are repre-
sentative of the entering air conditions encountered in many com-

other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_7d1d6242cd470e35", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coils", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "hydrophilic_coil_resistance", "title": "Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coils", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil."}, "evidence": "Therefore, final selection involves
economic balancing of the initial co st of the coil against the operat-
ing costs of the coil geometry co mbinations available to adequately
meet the performa nce requirements.
The aluminum fin surfaces of new dehumidifying coils tend to in-
hibit condensate sheeting action until they have aged for a year. Hy-
drophilic aluminum fin surface coatings reduce water droplet surface
tension, producing a more evenly dispersed wetted surface action at
initial start-up. Manufacturers have tried different methods of apply-
ing such coatings, including dippi ng the coil into a tank, coating the
fin stock material, or subjecting the material to a chemical etchingprocess. Tests have s hown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure
drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil..", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_0f2d0e09419d4908", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Freezing Prevention in Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_freezing_prevention", "title": "Freezing Prevention in Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated."}, "evidence": "In ho t, humid climates, the light-load
condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspond-
ingly lower proportion of sensible  heat. The result is higher dew
points in the conditioned spaces during light-load  conditions un-
less a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheator dual path) is used.
Fin surface freezing at light lo ads should be avoided. Freezing
occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature fallsbelow 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort
installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the
coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C satu rated; the exact value depends on
the design of the coil, its opera ting dew point, and the amount of
loading. With coil and condensing units to balance at low tempera-
tures at peak loads (not a custom ary design choice), freezing may
occur when load suddenly decreases.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}]}

Return JSON shape: {"verdicts":[{"candidate_id":"...","is_valid_hvac_knowledge":true,"reason":"short reason","category_if_not":null}]}
